Exploratory Data Analysis: Factors Influencing IMDb Movie Quality

Author

Group 10

Published

March 19, 2026

1 Introduction

The following analysis explores the internal characteristics of the movie dataset to identify potential predictors for our Generalized Linear Model (GLM). We specifically focus on whether a movie achieves an IMDb rating greater than 7.

2 Exploratory Analysis

2.1 Data Summary & Descriptive Statistics

To understand the central tendency and dispersion of our movie features, we generated a summary table for numerical variables. As presented in Table 1, the dataset covers a wide range of years and budgets.

The diversity of movie genres is another key aspect of our dataset. Table 2 shows the count and percentage of movies within each genre category after cleaning the missing values.

By reviewing Table 1, the variance of votes is exceptionally high compared to other predictors. This extreme dispersion indicates a “long-tail” distribution where a few extremely popular films receive a disproportionate amount of audience engagement, illustrating the need for a further transformation to stabilize the variable for the GLM. Furthermore, Table 2 highlights that Drama and Action are the most two prevalent categories in our sample. In contrast, Romance represents the smallest segment of the sample. This distribution reflects the producer’s focus on mainstream genres but suggests that the model’s predictive power for specific genres like Romance may be more limited.

2.2 Class Distribution

We examine the balance of our target variable (movie quality). As shown in Figure 1, we can observe the frequency of movies categorized as “Good” versus “Poor”.

Based on Figure 1, a significant class imbalance is observed in the target variable, with ‘Poor’ films (\(Rating < 7\)) outnumbering ‘Good’ films (\(Rating > 7\)) by nearly a factor of two. Because the model sees more examples of ‘Poor’ films, it might become better at identifying failures than predicting successes.

2.3 Numerical Variables vs Target

Analysis of the numerical predictors in Figure 2 reveals that while Year and Budget show nearly identical distributions across quality classes, Length and Votes offer more significant insights. Notably, “Poor” movies exhibit a “Mediocrity Trap,” with their durations tightly clustered around a standardized 100-minute mark, yet plagued by numerous extreme outliers (represented by the red markers in Figure 2). In contrast, “Good” movies—while also averaging near 100 minutes—display a much more flexible and varied distribution. Furthermore, because both the “Good” and “Poor” categories exhibit extreme right-skewness in their raw audience engagement metrics, we utilized a logarithmic transformation for the Votes variable. As shown in the red-highlighted outliers, this step was essential to stabilize the variance.

To address the extreme right-skewness inherent in audience engagement metrics, we compared the raw distribution of votes against a log-transformed version. As shown in Figure 3, the original votes variable is heavily compressed near zero with an expansive long tail, which would disproportionately bias our GLM. By applying a logarithmic transformation, we successfully achieved a more bell-shaped, stable distribution (Right), ensuring that the variance is consistent across both quality categories and making the data suitable for parametric modeling.

2.4 Categorical Analysis of Genre

As illustrated in Figure 4, genres such as Documentary and Short appear to have a significantly higher probability of being rated above 7.0 compared to Action or Drama films.

2.5 Multi-collinearity Check & Correlation

As shown in Figure 5, no predictors exhibit a correlation higher than 0.7, confirming that we can include all variables in the GLM.

3 Summary of Analysis

Our exploratory analysis shows several patterns in the data. First, there is a clear class imbalance: “Poor” films appear about twice as often as “Good” films, which creates a strong baseline for prediction. We also find that budget and production year have little relationship with film ratings. However, film length shows an interesting pattern. Lower-rated films tend to cluster around 100 minutes, while higher-rated films have a wider range of lengths.

In addition, the number of audience votes is highly right-skewed, so we applied a log transformation to stabilize the variance. After this transformation, higher-rated films still show consistently higher audience engagement. By combining these findings with differences across genres, we gain a clearer understanding of the factors related to movie quality and build a stronger foundation for our GLM model.
